hinge: Buccal recession with implant thread exposure requires soft tissue augmentation via connective tissue graft to cover the exposed threads and establish adequate keratinized tissue. Changing the crown or removing the implant are more invasive and should follow attempted grafting.

For the esthetic zone, a custom impression coping captures the individual emergence profile and soft tissue contours around the implant, providing the most accurate transfer to the master cast. Pick-up copings are for multi-unit divergent cases; plastic copings are standard but less precise.
Standard external hex implant connections have a hex height of 0.7 mm and width of 2.7 mm across flats. The 0.7 mm height is the standardized dimension.

For a 5 mm mesiodistal space (replacing a premolar), an implant with a screw-retained crown is the treatment of choice as it eliminates cement-related complications in small spaces and provides retrievability. Cement-retained would require at least 7-8 mm of space.

An implant with 5 mm probing depth, bleeding, and no excess cement present but with slight recession most likely has an overcontoured prosthetic contour that creates an unfavorable emergence profile, trapping plaque and causing peri-implant mucositis. Adjusting the crown contour (rather than retightening a screw or adjusting occlusion) addresses the etiology.

D4 bone (soft, poorly mineralized) is the worst bone quality for implants because it lacks cortical bone density and primarily consists of fine trabecular bone, resulting in poor primary stability at placement and higher failure rates.

The minimum number of implants for a mandibular overdenture is two, placed in the interforaminal region (canine positions), providing adequate retention and support while being minimally invasive and cost-effective.

A well-controlled diabetic patient is a suitable candidate for implant therapy. Diabetes is not an absolute contraindication when glycemic control is good (HbA1c <7%), and implants are preferred over removable prostheses for improved function and quality of life.

Peri-implant bone loss requires regeneration of the bony defect through bone grafting. Removing the implant or excess cement are for different clinical presentations. The question references a specific radiograph not available for audit.
Implants with diameter 3.5-4.5 mm are classified as regular/standard platform implants. Wide platform is >4.5 mm; narrow platform is <3.5 mm.
Implants between 3.5 mm and 4.1 mm fall into the regular/standard diameter category. This is the most commonly used implant diameter for posterior teeth.

For a cemented implant crown on a central incisor, the minimum mesiodistal space required is 7-8 mm to accommodate a standard 4 mm platform implant plus adequate restorative space and emergence profile.

The standard of care for edentulous mandible is an implant-supported overdenture with 2 implants as the minimum. McGill consensus states 2-implant overdenture is the minimum standard. 4-implant overdenture provides more support but is not the minimum standard.

A heavily destructed lateral incisor with failed post and core, endodontically treated, has poor long-term prognosis. Extraction and single implant replacement is the most predictable long-term treatment. Ortho extrusion with osseous surgery compromises root ratio; Maryland bridge has lower long-term survival.

With a fractured post and failed build-up on upper lateral incisor, re-doing the post and crown is the most conservative approach if adequate ferrule remains. Extraction and implant is irreversible; ortho extrusion requires additional procedures.

Bleeding on probing, pockets of 3 mm or more, and saucer-shaped bone loss on radiograph with metal exposure indicates peri-implantitis — progressive bone loss beyond initial remodeling with associated inflammation. Peri-mucositis has no bone loss.

A black/dark line visible around an anterior implant indicates a titanium abutment showing through thin peri-implant mucosa. Zirconia abutments are white/ceramic and eliminate the dark shadow, providing superior esthetics in anterior implant cases.

In a two-stage procedure with one failed implant (mobility with crestal bone loss) and one healthy implant, the mobile/failed implant should be removed. The healthy implant should not be loaded prematurely by connecting to a failing implant.

Posterior maxilla has poor bone quality (D3/D4) and insufficient primary stability is the predominant cause of implant failure in this region. High occlusal forces contribute but lack of primary stability at placement is the immediate technical cause.
After the first year of uneventful implant follow-up, recall intervals of 5-6 months are appropriate for maintenance and monitoring of peri-implant health in a compliant patient.

The distance from the papilla tip to the crestal bone for a single implant is approximately less than 5 mm. When this distance is maintained, the interdental papilla can fill the embrasure space fully.
Anterior maxillary implants should be placed slightly lateral to the midline to avoid the incisive canal and nasopalatine neurovascular bundle, which runs through the midline of the anterior maxilla.

Osseointegration in the maxillary posterior region requires approximately 6 months of healing due to the poorer bone quality (D3/D4) compared to mandibular bone. Mandibular implants typically require 3-4 months.

The upper canine is a corner tooth subjected to greater lateral forces during excursive movements due to canine guidance. A FPD replacing an upper canine experiences more lateral loading stress than a lower canine replacement.

For a thin gingival phenotype, using a smaller cord minimizes trauma to the delicate tissue during retraction while still achieving adequate sulcus exposure for an accurate impression. Gingivectomy or crown lengthening would further reduce already thin tissue.

PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate) provisional materials generate significant exothermic heat during polymerization that can damage the vital pulp if used directly on a tooth preparation without adequate cooling or a separating medium. Bis-acrylic generates less heat.

Flux in soldering dissolves/precipitates surface oxides, prevents further oxidation during heating, and improves the flow and wetting of solder by reducing surface tension, allowing the solder to flow into the joint by capillary action.

Equigingival margins are the best compromise between esthetics and health — they conceal the margin line slightly while still allowing for easy cleaning and avoiding biologic width violation. Supragingival is healthiest but least esthetic; subgingival is most esthetic but can violate biologic width.

Clenching is a parafunctional habit — non-physiologic, non-masticatory tooth contact. Chewing is a functional activity; erosion and abfraction are lesion types resulting from chemical and mechanical factors respectively.

For a bruxer with mild attrition, linea alba, and rough teeth surfaces, the immediate management involves educating the patient about the parafunctional habit and constructing a night guard to protect teeth from further wear and prevent symptoms. Smoothing roughness is symptomatic only.
